justification: The Enochic Watchers-Prometheus parallel concerns Jewish apocalyptic literature's mythological borrowing, not Jesus's titles, kingdom theology, or the Greco-Christian royal framework ch3 argues. Ch3 does not discuss 1 Enoch or the Book of Watchers. No other chapter inventory covers this specific parallel.

The finding concerns 1 Enoch Similitudes' influence on Matthew's Son of Man throne imagery. Ch3's Son of Man discussion (lines 422-444) maps the title to the Aegean representative-man concept, not Enochic traditions. This is a Jewish apocalyptic lineage argument outside ch3's Greco-Christian royal framework.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers Philo's Logos as 'firstborn son of God' and John's Gospel mirroring Philo. Ch4 item 35 covers the 'Dionysian Gospel' with water-to-wine and Dionysian parallels. Both Logos-Philo and Dionysus parallels are present in the book.

Ch3 does not argue that the Christian Trinity is a modified neoplatonic triad (One/Nous/Psyche) with hierarchical emanations collapsed into co-equality. The chapter discusses Platonic influence on the soul (lines 127-144) and Logos theology, but the specific structural argument about Trinity as bastardized neoplatonic triad is absent from all chapter inventories.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) explicitly includes Josephus proclaiming Vespasian as ruler foretold in Jewish prophecy, Yohanan ben Zakai greeting Vespasian as king, and Suetonius/Tacitus confirming Vespasian identified with Jewish oracle. The Flavian messianic propaganda is directly covered.

Ch3 argues Christian symbols connect to Hellenistic royal insignia (lines 610-619, ichthus in lines 422-444). Ch6 item 5 mentions anchor/fish on Flavian coinage generically. But the specific claim that the anchor-and-fish symbol appears identically on Titus coins — making Titus's and Jesus's symbols 'one and the same' — is a specific numismatic datum not present in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) argues 'Son of God' was the standard Hellenistic ruler title, and item 2 (lines 22-57) lists Augustus as divi filius. But the specific evidence that Roman adoption was more important than biological descent — Tiberius not biological child of Augustus, Augustus not biological child of Caesar — showing adoption-based succession as the norm, is new illustrative evidence not in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) explicitly covers the argument that the opening of Mark mimics imperial cult language, with evidence including Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals, and cities hailing emperors as soter. The Priene Calendar parallel to Mark's opening is the same argument and same evidence class already present.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly covers early Christian apologists comparing Jesus to Greek divine figures, citing Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22 listing Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus. The Apollonius of Tyana parallel is an additional example of the same argument class, but the argument (Son of God applied to Greco-Roman figures) and the comparative evidence type (named pagan parallels) are already present. Apollonius specifically is not named, making this: new_evidence_existing_argument.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) argues titles were pre-existing political terms. The specific argument that Homer's anointing narrative (Odyssey 19, Eurycleia washing Odysseus's feet) was deliberately imitated by Mark's Gospel anointing scene is not in ch3. Ch4 does not list this specific Homeric parallel either. The Homeric mimesis argument uses literary evidence not present in the book, though the broader argument (Gospels used Greek literary frameworks) exists in ch4.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) argues 'Son of God' was standard Hellenistic ruler title. Ehrman's specific framing — Caesar's adoption of Octavian where adopted son became Augustus while biological son Caesarion became 'a nobody,' and Mark's Jesus as 'adopted son of God' — provides a specific adoption-over-biology example with Caesarion contrast not present in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers the argument with Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to Greek divine figures including Heracles and Asclepius. Miller's description of the demigod tradition as a 'Hall of Fame' honorific system restates the same argument (Son of God as Hellenistic honorific, not literal biology) already in the chapter.

Ch3 does not specifically discuss the 'missing body' as the linchpin signal of divine translation in the Hercules and Romulus traditions. Ch2 item 68 discusses empty tomb objections but from a different angle (survival thesis). The specific typological evidence — missing body as the recognized Hellenistic signal of divine elevation — is new evidence for the existing argument about resurrection as political-theological legitimacy.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly cites Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22 comparing Jesus to Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus. Miller citing Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to Hercules, Dioscuri, Asclepius, Romulus is the same source and same argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 6 (lines 51-53) covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula applied at annunciation, baptism, and resurrection. The finding that different gospels mark Jesus as Son of God at different moments (Mark at baptism, Matthew/Luke at genealogy) restates the same three-event investiture argument with Greek/Roman parallels already present in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) explicitly argues basileia tou theou was the technical term for Hellenistic divine monarchies, and item 42 (lines 697-728) presents NT evidence for earthly restoration of sovereignty. The finding's framing of 'kingdom of God' as 'dictatorship on behalf of the peasantry replacing Rome' restates the political-institutional kingdom argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 16 covers dying-and-rising patterns implicitly through resurrection as political-theological legitimacy. But the specific argument that across Inanna/Baal/Dionysus traditions, the death-and-resurrection is situated within contexts of succession struggle and control of the universe (kingship) — with 'succession' as the primary motif — is specific comparative evidence not present in ch3's existing evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) argues titles were pre-existing political terms and item 20 covers Son of God as Hellenistic ruler title. But the specific evidence of Caesarea Philippi — Jesus confronting competing Son of God claims at a site hosting temples to both Pan and Augustus Caesar, asking 'Who do you think I am?' amid rival divine titles — is a specific geographic-theological datum not in ch3's evidence.

Plutarch's account of Mithras as 'mediator' between Ahura Mazda and Ahriman, predating Christian use of identical mediator language for Jesus, is neither an argument nor evidence present in any chapter inventory. Ch3 does not discuss Mithraic theology or mediator language. No cross-chapter inventory covers this Zoroastrian-Greek mediator parallel.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) argues Son of God was standard Hellenistic ruler title. Price's specific evidence — sacred king traditions where kings undergo ritual humiliation and death-resurrection cycles as annual renewal ceremonies, with the king as 'earthly avatar of the king of the gods' — adds specific ritual-cycle evidence not present in ch3's existing evidence for this argument.

Ch3 inventory item 40 (lines 620-633) covers celestial signs confirming divine kingship, including Augustus's birth star and Julius Caesar's deification comet. But the specific parallel to Virgil's Aeneid guiding-star narrative — magi traveling east to west led by a star to find a new king, matching the Aeneas story — is a specific literary parallel not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 40 (lines 620-633) explicitly includes 'Augustus: birth represented as new star; death/deification of Julius Caesar' and the Suetonius reference to celestial portents. The finding about comets/new stars as markers of apotheosis and divine kingship, specifically Julius Caesar's comet, restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 13 (lines 272-287) covers the Christian halo as solar disk connecting to Ra and pharaonic imagery. But the specific iconographic evidence of Jesus depicted with solar halo surrounded by twelve apostles paralleling Sol Invictus/Helios surrounded by twelve zodiac signs is a specific visual-comparative datum not present in ch3's halo evidence.

Hellenized Horus (Harpocrates) depicted as infant with three divine visitors (Zeus, Hermes, Hera) and star above, providing Greco-Roman precedent for nativity narrative motifs, is not present in any chapter inventory. Ch3 discusses nativity star (item 40) but not Harpocrates iconography. No cross-chapter inventory covers this parallel.

Ch3 discusses the twelve apostles in the context of kingdom governance (item 42, twelve thrones judging twelve tribes). But the specific argument that the number twelve functions as a cosmological unit in the Greek zodiac system, with earliest Christian artwork deliberately correlating twelve apostles to twelve zodiac signs, is a specific numerological-cosmological argument adding new evidence to the existing twelve-apostles framework.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) covers Son of God as standard Hellenistic ruler title with Alexander and Ptolemies as sons of God. The finding that Julius Caesar was 'the first man God to be worshipped officially by the Romans since Romulus' and was 'son of a god' as descendant of Venus restates the same argument with Roman examples already implicit in the Augustus divi filius evidence (item 2).

Ch3 inventory item 49 (lines 871-904) argues Jesus's love ethic was political strategy for rebuilding civic solidarity. But the specific parallel evidence — Julius Caesar's agrarian reforms helping the poor, forgiving enemies, giving them political office — as a precedent for Jesus's kingdom teachings is new comparative evidence not in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 40 (lines 620-633) explicitly includes 'Augustus: birth represented as new star; death/deification of Julius Caesar' and Suetonius on portents, post-mortem appearances. The finding about Julius Caesar's apotheosis with a comet/star seen in the heavens is already present in ch3's star-birth evidence.

Ch2 inventory item 30 covers euangelion as a political term for royal proclamation. Ch3 does not trace the etymology further. The specific evidence that the root of 'evangelion' connects to Hermes as Zeus's 'faithful messenger' (angelos) adds a mythological-etymological datum not present in the book's existing euangelion discussion.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) argues basileia tou theou was the Hellenistic political term, and the chapter connects kingdom theology to political institutions. The finding's specific mechanism — that pacifistic Christianity emerged as a deliberate theological reinterpretation of militant Messianism specifically in response to Jewish military defeat — adds a historical-causal mechanism not in ch3's existing evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christian apologists arguing Jesus's divine sonship used the same categories as Greek divine sonship, citing Justin Martyr listing Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus. Miller's argument that Jesus was elevated to Son of God through adopting Greco-Roman theological forms (ascension, virgin birth) while non-Greco-Roman traditions did not develop these attributes restates the same argument already in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) argues Son of God as standard Hellenistic ruler title. The specific evidence of Alexander the Great's divine conception through serpent impregnation (making him a demigod) is a specific mythological datum not explicitly present in ch3's Alexander evidence, which focuses on Zeus-Ammon at Siwa and coins (item 35, lines 574-580).

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) argues titles were pre-existing political terms Greeks already used for rulers, and item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christians arguing Jesus used same categories as Greek divine sonship. The finding that 'Son of God' would have registered unmistakably to Greek-speaking audiences as a demigod/divine figure, not a Jewish messianic category, restates arguments already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) argues basileia tou theou was the technical term for Hellenistic divine monarchies. The global Q&A explicitly rejects the 'mystery cult' framing and reinterprets competition with cults as imperial cult under foreign rule. The finding about Christianity competing alongside mystery cults is addressed by the Q&A decision: extract institutional data, reject mystery cult conclusion.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers the Logos concept including Philo's development, and ch4 item 18 covers Philo's Logos theology as pre-existing high Christology. The finding about John's Prologue employing Platonic demiurge concepts restates the Logos-as-Greek-philosophy argument already present in ch3's Logos section.

Ch3 does not discuss the 11Q Melchizedek scroll. The Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah concept is covered (inventory item 17, lines 345-421). But the specific evidence that 11Q Melchizedek portrays Melchizedek as an eschatological savior who judges humanity and provides atonement — christological roles — is a specific DSS text not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly cites Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22 comparing Jesus to 'sons of Zeus.' Miller citing Justin comparing Jesus to sons of Zeus and Caesars with eyewitness testimony is the same source and argument.

Ch3 does not enumerate the specific set of Hellenistic 'translation fable' literary signals (missing body, ascension, Son of God claims, eyewitness testimony, taken away in cloud) as a structured set of conventions signaling divine translation. The argument that resurrection is a political-theological legitimacy claim exists (implicit in kingdom theology), but the specific literary-convention mechanism ('translation fable signals') is new evidence.

Ch4 inventory item 35 explicitly covers 'the earliest stratum of John reads as a Dionysian Gospel built on deliberate imitation of Euripides Bacchae.' Ch4 item 15 covers water-to-wine as Dionysian. The finding about water-to-wine at Cana paralleling Dionysus temple rituals and Jesus as 'the true vine' being a Dionysian proclamation is already covered in ch4.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers Logos in John from Greek philosophy. The specific evidence from Margaret Barker distinguishing 'Son of theos/Elohim' from 'Son of kyrios/Lord' — Jesus is called son of theos but never son of kyrios, which is difficult to explain if Lord simply means God — is a specific linguistic-theological datum not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 35 (lines 574-580) explicitly covers Alexander claiming divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon at Siwa, with evidence from Plutarch. The finding restates this with 'Alexander was son of Zeus-Ammon, the combination of two gods' — same argument and evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers Greek divine figures compared to Jesus. But Litwa's specific description of Heracles's apotheosis mechanism — mortal flesh 'drips off,' divine portion from Zeus 'floats up' — as a model for divine-human transformation is specific evidence not in ch3's Heracles references.

The distinction between 'epiphany' (dominant Greco-Roman model: temporary divine appearance in flesh) and 'incarnation' (later Christian theological development) is a specific methodological argument not present in any chapter inventory. Ch3 discusses God manifested in flesh (Epiphanes, lines 505-512) but does not make this epiphany-vs-incarnation distinction.

Simon of Samaria calling himself 'the Standing One' (histemi/hastemi) as a Jewish adaptation of Greek deification language — declaring eternality using Semitic rather than Greek 'immortal' terminology — is not present in any chapter inventory. Neither the specific figure (Simon Magus) nor the linguistic mechanism (Semitic eternality language adapting Greek concepts) appears.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) covers Augustus as divi filius. But the specific parallel between Luke's addressee 'Theophilos' (god-lover) and Augustus's title 'divi filius' (son of the divine) — both beginning with 'god' — as deliberate engagement with imperial theology is a specific literary-structural observation not in ch3's evidence. Ch5 item 40 covers Luke as Aegean gospel but does not note this Theophilos-divi filius parallel.

Ch3 inventory item 39 (lines 610-619) covers inscriptions and coins as evidence of god-king political theology, including 'Coins in Jesus's lifetime: theos sebastos Kaisar.' The finding about Roman imperial cult with organized emperor worship restates evidence already present in ch3's coins/inscriptions section.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) argues basileia tou theou was the technical Hellenistic term, and item 42 covers NT evidence for earthly sovereignty restoration. The finding about messianic theology emphasizing the Messiah as military-political conqueror who will overthrow empires restates the political-institutional kingdom argument already in ch3.

Ch3 does not discuss the Q document or its theology of divine vindication/exaltation as distinct from bodily resurrection. The finding that Q emphasizes glorification and exaltation (like Elijah, Moses, Enoch) rather than resurrection adds new evidence about earliest Jesus tradition framing legitimacy through vindication rather than resurrection — supporting the existing argument about political-theological legitimacy but through a different mechanism.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers Jesus as Logos from Greek philosophy, and ch4 item 35 covers the Dionysian Gospel. The finding about John presenting Jesus as 'the logos,' 'one in the lap of the father,' with Dionysian parallels restates arguments already present across ch3 and ch4.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) explicitly covers Philo's Logos described as 'firstborn son of God' (De Confusione Linguarum 146) and John's Gospel mirroring Philo's development. Price discussing Philo's Logos with identical titles ('son of god,' 'heavenly high priest') paralleling Pauline epistles and John restates evidence already present.

Paul's use of Philo's heavenly Adam prototype in 1 Corinthians 15 concerns Pauline theology and its Greek philosophical sources. This belongs in chapter 5 ('Pauline Epistles to All Nations'), which covers Paul's cosmology (ch5 item 43: 'Paul and Luke employ Greek cosmology, Platonic-Stoic metaphysics') and Paul's theological frameworks. Ch3 does not discuss 1 Corinthians 15 or the heavenly Adam concept.

Ch3 argues about political-institutional delegation (apostoloi as royal emissaries, item 43; kingdom as political institution). Carrier's framing of Jesus's authority as delegated power from God — 'God can assign those rights and powers to anyone he wants' — adds a specific theological-institutional framework (delegation model) as evidence for the existing political-institutional argument.

Carrier's subordinationist framework for Paul (Philippians 2 showing Jesus is not identical to God, God delegates authority to angelic agents) concerns Pauline Christology specifically. This belongs in chapter 5, which covers Paul's theological framework and cosmic salvation (ch5 items 43-44). Ch3 does not discuss Philippians 2 or Pauline subordinationism.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers Philo's Logos theology and John's Gospel mirroring it. Price's discussion of Paul having 'a celestial Christ' paralleling Philo's 'heavenly Logos' restates the Philo-Logos connection already in ch3. The celestial Christ concept is implicit in the existing Logos framework.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly cites Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22 comparing Jesus to Greek divine figures. The finding about Justin comparing Jesus to Mercury/Hermes (called the Logos, same as Jesus in John) and noting Jesus shares attributes with 'sons of Jupiter' restates the same source and argument. Justin's specific mention of Hermes as Logos adds minor detail but the argument and source are present.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) argues titles were pre-existing political terms used for rulers. The specific evidence from Philo of Alexandria describing Augustus — 'who calmed the torrential storms on every side, who healed pestilences common to Greeks and barbarians' — showing identical miraculous attributes ascribed to living rulers, is a specific textual datum not in ch3's existing Augustus evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers Heracles and Greek divine parallels. The specific evidence that Josephus's original Greek text describes Heracles using 'eggersis' (resurrection) — the same term used in the NT — demonstrating resurrection terminology was applied to Hellenistic deified figures, is a specific linguistic datum not in ch3's existing evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 covers Greek divine parallels to Jesus. The specific example of Antinous (Hadrian's lover) being 'clothed like Osiris,' becoming 'Osirantinous the reborn and everlasting,' with others being initiated into his cult for immortalization — a 2nd century CE real-person deification — is a specific historical example not in ch3's evidence.

MacDonald's identification of Homeric literary parallels in Mark's Gospel (temple cleansing merging Homer's Odysseus cleansing his father's house with Jerusalem temple cleansing) concerns Gospel literary construction. This belongs in chapter 4 ('Gospels as Historically Reliable Sources'), which covers Mark's literary techniques (ch4 item 38: Mark's introduction as coronation narrative). Ch3 does not discuss Homeric mimesis in Mark.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) extensively covers John the Baptist as priestly co-heir with anointing/succession context, including DSS dual messiah (1QS IX:11), baptism as priestly initiation, and Jesus identifying John as Elijah. McGrath's discussion of John's role in anointing figures as kings, paralleling Samuel, restates the anointing-succession argument already present.

Ch3 implicitly connects resurrection to political legitimacy through kingdom theology. The specific evidence that Gospel resurrection eyewitness claims follow the pattern of Greco-Roman apotheosis traditions (Romulus, Julius Caesar) — and that without memetic connection to Romulus, there would be no necessity for eyewitness claims — is a specific literary-structural argument adding new evidence.

Mason's observation that monogamy became standard under Greek and Roman influence concerns social practices, not Jesus's titles, kingdom theology, or the Greco-Christian royal framework that ch3 argues. No chapter inventory covers this specific topic.

Ch3 inventory item 31 (lines 546-552) covers Soter (Savior) as the most common title for Hellenistic rulers. The global Q&A rejects the 'mystery cult' framing. The finding about Hellenistic savior gods offering eternal life through ritual initiation (Bacchic mysteries) is addressed by the Q&A decision: the data is real but the mystery cult framing is rejected in favor of imperial cult under foreign rule.

Ch3 inventory item 30 (lines 535-544) covers the Good Shepherd as royal title with Hermes Criophorus as earliest Jesus imagery. The finding adds the specific argument that 'sons of God, demigods' were also good shepherds (Hermes), showing porosity between shepherd-king traditions across Greek and Near Eastern cultures. This adds the demigod-shepherd connection as new evidence.

Ch3 does not explicitly discuss apotheosis as the mechanism underlying resurrection (empty tomb = body disappears = deified). The specific evidence that empty tomb/disappeared body signaled apotheosis and deification in Greco-Roman literature — with the women's panic at the empty tomb matching established literary conventions — is new evidence for the implicit argument about resurrection as political-theological legitimacy.

Ch3 does not explicitly discuss Paul's resurrection claims as operating within Hellenistic 'legend' and 'topos' traditions. The finding that even Paul's resurrection claims should be understood within Hellenistic narrative frameworks (not purely Jewish categories) adds new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Zinner's demonstration that Paul adopted Stoic understanding of natural law vs written law, appropriating Jewish scriptures to support Greek philosophical frameworks, concerns Pauline theology specifically. This belongs in chapter 5, which covers Paul's Greek philosophical frameworks (ch5 item 43: 'Paul and Luke employ Greek cosmology, Platonic-Stoic metaphysics'). Ch3 does not discuss Paul's Stoic law theory.

The specific argument that the Sermon on the Mount was constructed from Stoic principles mediated through Philo of Alexandria, not from Jewish tradition — with loving your enemy as 'right out of stoicism' built on Philo's Stoic ethics — is not present in any chapter inventory. Ch3 item 49 discusses love ethic as political strategy but does not trace it to Stoic/Philonic origins. This is a new argument with new evidence.

The finding that Matthew deliberately removes the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4, 'Hear O Israel, the Lord your God is one') from the great commandment — stripping the explicitly Jewish monotheistic declaration while retaining ethical content — is not present in any chapter inventory. This is a specific act of de-Judaization at the compositional level, a new argument with specific textual evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers Philo's Logos theology and its role as intermediary between Greek philosophy and Christian theology. Ch3 inventory item 8 covers the Father-God in Greek philosophical categories. The finding about Philo as mediator of Greco-Christian thought, with his allegorical method adopted by Paul and opposed by James, restates the Philo-as-bridge argument already implicit in the Logos and Father sections.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) explicitly covers Jesus as Logos anchored in Greek philosophy and Hellenistic Jewish philosophy, with Philo's development. Mendes's point that John's theology presents Jesus as Divine Logos 'anchored in Greek philosophy and Hellenistic Jewish philosophy' restates the existing argument.

Ch3 discusses Jesus as God manifested in the flesh (Epiphanes, item 28, lines 505-512). But John's specific deification theology — where believers become divine through accepting Jesus's indwelling presence (theosis) — is a specific theological concept not present in ch3's evidence. The theosis concept adds new evidence to the Hellenistic divine-transformation argument.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly cites Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22 acknowledging Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus.' Miller documenting that Justin acknowledged Jesus's exaltation narrative was 'not new' compared to demigod legends restates the same source and argument.

Ch3 argues Jesus's titles should be understood through Greco-Roman frameworks. Miller's specific methodological claim — that 'conventional cultural grammar determines the correct cultural semiotic interpretation' — adds a theoretical framework for why Greco-Roman context is essential. This is a methodological articulation supporting the existing argument but not present as specific evidence in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christian apologists arguing Jesus used Greek divine sonship categories, citing Justin Martyr on Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus. Ehrman's point that divine-human births were common Greco-Roman motifs (Hercules from Jupiter and Alcmena) and Christians portrayed Jesus 'in a way that sons of God are portrayed' restates the same argument.

Ch3 inventory item 21 (lines 476-485) covers 'Son of Father' with Plutarch's dynamis/pneuma and Luke 1:35 using same vocabulary. But the specific Lukan formulation — 'the spirit of the most high will come upon you, the power of the most high will overshadow you, so that the one born of you will be called holy the son of God' — framed explicitly as parallel to divine impregnation (Jupiter fathering offspring) is more specific than ch3's existing evidence. However, ch3 item 21 already includes Luke 1:35 with power and spirit. Verdict: covered.

Ch3's entire framework argues that understanding Jesus requires engagement with the broader Hellenistic context. Tabor's emphasis that scholars must study Hellenistic mystery religions and parallel sources restates the book's foundational methodological position. This is a methodological endorsement of the book's approach, not new evidence or argument.

Ch3 inventory item 32 (lines 553-562) explicitly covers Kyrios (Lord) as standard imperial honorific with 'Kyrios Kaisar — Lord Caesar.' Tabor's point that 'Lord' (Kyrios) was routinely applied to Caesar restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers Greek divine sonship parallels. The concept of apotheosis (human deification/exaltation to heavenly status) as widespread in the Greco-Roman world is covered by the book's argument about Son of God as Hellenistic royal title. Tabor noting Eastern Orthodox Church maintained this framework adds a minor detail but the argument is present.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) explicitly includes Philo describing Logos as 'firstborn son of God' (De Confusione Linguarum 146), and the Logos section covers the concept as 'firstborn son of God,' 'agent of creation,' within Hellenistic theological categories. The finding restates this.

Ch3 covers worship of Jesus as divine figure implicitly through the title sections. The specific Pliny the Younger testimony (c. 112 AD) documenting early Christians singing hymns 'to Christ as to a God' is referenced in ch2 item 48 but not in ch3's evidence. As external documentary evidence for Christ's divine status in early Christian practice, it adds new evidence to ch3's existing argument about Son of God as divine title.

Ch3 does not explicitly discuss 'divine translation narratives' as a structured category of Mediterranean literature. The finding about empty tomb narratives fitting established Greco-Roman literary patterns of missing bodies implying divine exaltation adds new evidence (the structured 'translation fable' category) for the existing argument about Greco-Christian frameworks.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) explicitly cites Justin Martyr First Apology 21-22. The finding quoting Justin: 'when we affirm that Jesus Christ died, rose, and ascended to heaven, we say nothing different from what you believe about those you call the sons of Zeus' restates the same source already present.

The identification of specific narrative tropes shared between Gospel appearance accounts and Greco-Roman theophanic literature — hidden identity, cognitive resistance, token display, recognition, sudden disappearance — as a structured literary pattern is not present in any chapter inventory. This is a specific literary-structural argument with new evidence about how Gospel narratives were composed.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Messiah as authentic Judean royal title with Herod as 'King of the Jews.' The finding about Matthew's emphasis on Bethlehem as a direct counter-claim to Herod's own Davidic Messianic legitimacy adds new evidence (Herod claiming Davidic Messianic legitimacy, Matthew's Massacre of Innocents as Jewish reaction to that claim) not present in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christian comparisons to Greek divine figures. The specific evidence that Lactantius (4th century) recognized Hermes Trismegistus teaching about God having a son — 'Hermes said everything about God the father and much about the son' — is a specific Hermetic-patristic datum not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) explicitly covers Jesus teaching the Greek (Platonic) model of immortal soul vs Hebrew nefesh. Ehrman's point about Christian theology synthesizing Jewish apocalyptic and Greek Platonic frameworks about soul/afterlife restates the argument already present in ch3's soul doctrine section.

Ch3 inventory item 21 (lines 476-485) discusses 'only-begotten of the Father.' The Nicene Creed's specific formulation 'begotten not made, of one substance with the Father' using Hellenistic philosophical terminology is a specific historical outcome of the royal succession language argument. Ch3 does not cite the Nicene Creed formulation as evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss the Nero redivivus legend. The finding that Revelation engages with the widespread belief that Nero would return from death — paralleling Christ's resurrection and return within imperial political frameworks — is new evidence for the argument about resurrection as political-theological legitimacy. Ch5 item 53 covers Revelation as imperial restoration but not the Nero return parallel specifically.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Messiah/Christ as royal title with anointing. But the Gospel of Philip's specific description of crismation — 'the crism is superior to baptism, for from the crism we were called Christian' and 'the father anointed the son, and the son anointed the apostles, and the apostles anointed us' — is a specific primary source with succession-chain evidence not in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 6 (lines 51-53) covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula applied at baptism with 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you.' The Gospel of Philip describing Jesus becoming God's son through baptismal transformation (divine adoption via logos and Holy Spirit) restates the baptism-as-investiture argument already in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 11 covers the Lord's Prayer avoiding Jewish covenant markers. The Didache containing an alternate eucharistic theology that assigns Greek political meaning to Jewish symbols (wine as David's vine, bread as community unity) without reference to crucifixion or Last Supper is new evidence. Ch3 mentions the Didache as earliest liturgical manual (item 12, line 206) but not its eucharistic theology.

Ch3 inventory item 34 (lines 570-573) covers Light of the World with solar metaphors. The specific evidence of Constantine describing the Christian God with solar metaphors ('most brilliant beams,' 'savior of all extending light to all') syncretizing Sol Invictus and Christian theology is new evidence not in ch3's Light of the World section.

Ch3 inventory item 39 (lines 610-619) covers coins and inscriptions as evidence of god-king political theology. The specific evidence of Constantine's Chi-Rho used as imperial insignia on coins, helmets, battle standards, and statue inscriptions is new evidence. Ch6 item 20 covers Chi-Rho as imperial monogram but ch3 does not include this evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) includes Augustus as divi filius. The specific evidence that Domitian minted coins in Alexandria with 'Son of God' title, and Christians invented the ichthus acrostic as imitation or parody of this imperial title, is new evidence. Ch3 mentions the ichthus acrostic (item 18, lines 422-444) but not its connection to Domitian's Alexandrian coinage.

Ch3 inventory item 6 (lines 51-53) covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula with Luke 1:32-35 as evidence. The finding that Luke's narrative assigns Jesus 'son of the most high' and heir to 'throne of his father David ruling over Israel forever' restates evidence already in ch3's annunciation evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 35 (lines 574-580) covers Alexander's divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon. Ch3 item 21 (lines 476-485) includes Plutarch's dynamis/pneuma for divine conception. Tabor acknowledging the Zeus-Hercules parallel while noting Christian account differs restates comparisons already present.

Ch3 does not explicitly use the Greek technical term 'apotheosis' for Mark's resurrection understanding. The finding that Mark employed apotheosis — spiritual exaltation rather than physical corpse resurrection — as the framework for resurrection, using explicitly Hellenistic vocabulary, adds specific Markan evidence to the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 discusses Platonic soul doctrine (item 10, lines 127-144) but does not apply the Greek term 'metamorphosis' to Paul's resurrection theology. The finding that Paul conceived resurrection as metamorphosis — transformation of 'dust atom' into 'lifegiving spirit' — using Greek terminology, adds specific Pauline-Greek evidence to the existing argument.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) extensively covers John the Baptist as priestly co-heir with the Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah concept (1QS IX:11, Damascus Document). The finding about the Last Supper with DSS evidence showing priestly hierarchy (priest blessing first, then Messiah) restates the dual messiah institutional evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers the priestly-royal institutional division with DSS dual messiah evidence. The brief comment about 'one is a priest and one is of a royal line' restates the dual-messiah argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) argues basileia tou theou was the Hellenistic political term, and item 42 covers earthly restoration of sovereignty. Tabor emphasizing the Kingdom of God with 'full political and social implications' restates the political-institutional kingdom argument already present.

Ch3 covers the Messiah title (item 19) and Son of God title (item 20) separately. Tabor's specific framing — that Paul transformed the original political understanding of Jesus as Davidic Messiah into a theology of Divine Son of God, marginalizing the political title — documents a specific theological shift not explicitly described in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christians arguing Jesus's divine sonship used Greek categories. Ch3 item 21 (lines 476-485) includes divine conception parallels. The finding about early Christians appropriating Greco-Roman divine birth conventions to affirm Jesus as 'both human and divine' restates arguments already present.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers early Christian comparisons to Greek divine figures. The Apollonius of Tyana parallel — born same time as Jesus, fathered by Zeus, healed sick, raised dead, ascended to heaven — is a specific example, but the argument class (Greco-Roman contemporaries with identical Son of God attributes) is already covered. Apollonius specifically is not named in ch3, making this borderline. However, the Justin Martyr source already covers the pattern. Verdict: new_evidence_existing_argument.

Ch3 inventory item 30 (lines 535-544) covers Good Shepherd with earliest Jesus images in pose of Hermes Criophorus. The finding adds the specific connection of Jesus to both Hermes Criophorus AND the god Attis — solar deity associations — providing additional iconographic evidence not in ch3's Good Shepherd section.

Ch3 does not explicitly use the term 'apotheosis' as the framework for Paul's resurrection theology. Tabor explicitly stating 'Resurrection is more apotheosis' — defining it as 'being taken up into the Heavenly world' — adds specific Greco-Roman theological vocabulary as evidence for the existing argument about Greco-Christian frameworks.

Ch3 does not discuss Paul's emphasis on 'glory' (doxa) as the central theological term for resurrection transformation. The finding about Paul emphasizing cosmic transformation to a 'glorious being' — contrasting with later physical resurrection — adds specific Pauline vocabulary evidence to the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 does not discuss the exclusivity of resurrection appearances to believers/disciples. The finding that resurrection encounters happened only to disciples, never to skeptics or outsiders, supports understanding resurrection as internal theological legitimacy claim but adds new evidence about the sociology of the claims.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) covers Jesus teaching the Greek Platonic model of immortal soul. Tabor tracing how Hellenistic/Platonic philosophy progressively shaped Jewish-Christian theological development, calling the immortal soul concept a 'foreign idea that infected religious thought,' restates the argument already in ch3's anthropology section.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers 'Christ' as existing Greek word meaning 'anointed' with the Hasmoneans as anointed priest-kings. Tabor emphasizing that anointing with oil creates and designates someone as 'messiah' ('a woman made Jesus the messiah') restates the anointing-as-royal-title argument already present.

Ch3's entire framework argues Jesus should be understood through a Greco-Christian lens. Tabor affirming Jesus as a 'Hellenistic guy' and 'stoic cynic philosopher type' restates the book's foundational thesis. This is methodological endorsement, not new evidence or argument.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) argues basileia tou theou was the Hellenistic political term. The finding about followers told not to revolt because 'the kingdom of God is right at hand' — apocalyptic pacifism as political strategy — restates the political kingdom argument. Ch3 item 49 (lines 871-904) covers love ethic as political strategy.

Ch3 inventory item 4 covers Dead Sea Scroll 4Q246 with Son of God/Son of Most High titles. But the specific 'Self-Glorification Hymn' text — a Teacher claiming 'none comparable to me in glory,' 'a mighty throne in the congregation of angels' — is a different DSS text not in ch3's evidence, providing new evidence for the royal-authority argument.

Ch3 inventory item 32 (lines 553-562) covers Kyrios as standard imperial honorific with Psalm 110:1 and the 'two Lords' text (Mark 12:36). The Adon (Lord) figure with enemies made a footstool is already present in ch3's Kyrios evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 4 covers DSS 4Q246. The finding that resurrection in Dead Sea Scrolls thought was understood specifically as 'apotheosis' — heavenly exaltation to divine status — adds the specific Greek theological term as the DSS community's framework, which is new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian argument.

Ch3 does not use the term 'apotheosis' for Jesus's resurrection. Tabor framing resurrection as apotheosis — 'being taken up into the Heavenly world' — with comparison to Romulus, adds specific Hellenistic theological vocabulary evidence for the existing framework argument. Multiple findings in this batch bring this same apotheosis evidence; it remains new because ch3 lacks it.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) covers kingdom of God as Hellenistic political term, and item 42 covers earthly sovereignty restoration. The finding about Jesus's royal donkey procession with crowds chanting 'Hosanna to the son of David' and 'blessed is the kingdom of our Father David' restates the political-institutional kingdom claim already present.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers 'King of the Jews as real royal title borne by Herod; charge for Jesus's execution.' The finding that to claim Messiahship was to declare oneself King, and Romans had 'zero tolerance for Messiahs' as 'seditious enemies of Rome,' restates evidence already present.

Tabor's point that Paul employed Platonic philosophical frameworks to transform earthly Messianic concepts into heavenly ones, and that Judaism was 'already thoroughly hellenized,' concerns Pauline theology specifically. This belongs in chapter 5, which covers Paul's Greek philosophical frameworks (ch5 items 43-44). While ch3 mentions Platonic influence, Paul's specific theological transformation method is ch5 material.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Messiah as Judean royal title with evidence including the 'Christ' translation from Hebrew to Greek. Eisenman's description of Palestinian messianic concepts translated into Greek 'Christ' for the Greco-Roman world — 'the minute you're talking about Christianity, you're talking about a Hellenized production' — restates the argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) extensively covers the Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah concept with 1QS IX:11, Damascus Document, and institutional roles. The finding about priestly Messiah as 'spiritual leader' and kingly Messiah as 'president/executive' with 'council of peace' restates the dual-messiah institutional argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 42 (lines 697-728) covers the kingdom as earthly sovereignty restoration, and the finding about Jesus establishing Capernaum as HQ for 'a provisional revolutionary government of the kingdom of God' with the Twelve on thrones judging the twelve tribes restates the political-institutional kingdom argument. This kingdom-as-government framework is already in ch3.

Paul thinking of himself as 'kind of a second Christ' with royal exaltation language ('sit at my right hand') extends Hellenistic royal language to Paul personally. This concerns Pauline self-understanding and belongs in chapter 5, which covers Paul's role as imperial envoy (ch5 items 4-6). Ch3 focuses on Jesus's titles, not Paul's self-understanding.

Ch3 inventory item 5 (lines 47-50) covers Greek divine figures compared to Jesus. The specific description of Romulus's apotheosis — 'taken up to heaven and his body melts away and he becomes glorious and shining like a star' — is a specific narrative detail not present in ch3's evidence, though the Romulus parallel is mentioned in other contexts.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) argues Son of God as standard Hellenistic ruler title. Litwa's specific comparison of Christian deification to Roman emperor deification — 'Paul spreads a gospel saying everybody you can become immortal just like the Roman Emperor or Heracles or Asclepius or Romulus' — adds a specific democratization-of-deification argument as new evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 41 covers kingdom of God as Hellenistic political term. The finding about salvation in Paul meaning achieving immortality and cosmic power — defining what a god is in the Greek-Roman world (deathless life + superhuman power) — adds specific content definition to the existing legitimacy argument.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution with earthly governance. The finding about Jesus's Council of Twelve as 'a government in exile' — 'putting his cabinet together to take over and rule the country' — restates the political-institutional kingdom argument already present in ch3.

Walsh describing Paul's cosmology as embedded in Stoic philosophy, with the Messiah 'marshalling this heavenly Army to bring in this new kingdom,' concerns Pauline theological frameworks. This belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43: Paul employs Greek cosmology). Ch3 discusses kingdom theology but not Paul's specific Stoic cosmological embedding.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah with '1QS IX:11 — messiahs of Aaron and Israel,' Damascus Document, and paired priestly/royal leadership. The finding about Malachi prophesying two distinct anointed messiahs in DSS versions restates the dual-messiah evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 4 (line 46) covers 4Q246 with 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High.' The finding about Deuteronomy 32:8 in DSS reading 'sons of God' (Bene Elohim, heavenly beings) instead of 'children of Israel' — contextualizing divine sonship in the textual tradition of Jesus's time — is a different DSS textual variant not in ch3's evidence.

Walsh describing Paul's cosmology as embedded in Stoic philosophy with kingdom theology belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43). Ch3 discusses kingdom theology but not Paul's specific Stoic cosmological framework.

Walsh explaining pneuma as physical substance within Stoic cosmology in Paul's theology belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43: Paul employs Greek cosmology, Platonic-Stoic metaphysics). Ch3 does not discuss pneuma as Stoic substance.

Walsh discussing mistranslation of Greek genitive constructions in Paul's letters (faithfulness of Jesus vs faith in Jesus) concerns Pauline textual interpretation. This belongs in chapter 5 or chapter 4. Ch5 item 44 discusses Paul's justification by faith as Greek civic-forensic language.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers 'King of the Jews as real royal title borne by Herod; charge for Jesus's execution.' The finding comparing 'two kings of the Jews' — Herod declared by Roman Senate vs Jesus declared by populace — restates the political kingship argument already present.

Ch2 inventory item 52 covers dynastic succession (James, Simon, Jude succeeding Jesus). The finding about James's succession as a 'caliphate' — formal political succession by bloodline — restates the dynastic succession argument already present in ch2. For ch3 specifically, item 17 covers John the Baptist's priestly role in the succession framework.

Ch3 inventory item 25 (lines 486-504) covers the Logos concept from Heraclitus, Stoics, Middle Platonists, through Philo to John. Tabor noting Paul's framework parallels Platonic philosophy with 'different spheres of God' restates the Platonic influence argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 33 (lines 563-569) covers Prince of Peace from Isaiah 9:6 as royal epithet. Tabor's analysis that Isaiah 9:6 designates the Messiah with divine attributes as royal title-names representing God's character (not claiming literal divinity) adds specific exegetical evidence for the title-as-designation argument, showing the titles were functional not ontological.

Ch3 inventory item 4 covers DSS 4Q246 with Son of God title. The 4Q521 scroll describing a pre-Christian Messiah who heals the sick, preaches good news to the poor, and raises the dead — matching exactly Jesus's response to John's disciples — is a different DSS text providing specific new evidence for the existing messianic titles argument.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers 'King of the Jews' as charge for execution. The finding about Romans having 'zero tolerance for Messiahs' as 'potentially seditious enemies of Rome' restates evidence already in ch3's Messiah section.

Paul's early understanding of resurrection as spiritual transformation rather than physical corpse resuscitation concerns Pauline theology. This belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43: Paul employs Greek cosmology). Ch3 discusses resurrection implicitly through kingdom theology but Paul's specific resurrection theology is ch5 material.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Messiah as political royal title and 'King of the Jews' as charge for execution. The finding about Jesus identifying as 'Israel's king' and Romans crucifying him 'for claiming to be a king' restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution with earthly restoration. The Letter of James's political-social understanding of the kingdom focused on earthly justice and governance adds perspective but the argument (kingdom as political-social institution, 'on earth as in heaven') is already in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution. Jesus's royal entry procession fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy (donkey procession, temple cleansing as 'prophetic pantomime') restates the political kingdom argument. The temple action is covered in ch3 item 15 (lines 332-343, Jesus's conflicts over authority).

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution with earthly restoration. The finding about Jesus planning 'complete overthrow of all that Rome represented' based on Hebrew prophecy about kingdoms becoming 'kingdom of God and of his Messiahs' restates the political kingdom argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers baptism as priestly initiation of royal messiah. The Ebionite concept of the Holy Spirit 'hastening through the ages' to rest on Jesus, with the dove at baptism representing permanent dwelling of the spirit, restates the baptism-as-investiture argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 42 (lines 697-728) covers the kingdom as earthly sovereignty restoration. The finding about the Twelve as 'provisional government' with 'inner cabinet' structured to govern twelve tribes restates the political-institutional kingdom argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 4 (line 46) explicitly covers Dead Sea Scroll 4Q246 applying 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High' to an eschatological ruler before Christian usage. The finding restates this same evidence.
Ch3 inventory item 2 (lines 22-57) includes Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals. The finding noting that Octavian called himself 'Son of God' restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 42 (lines 697-728) covers kingdom as earthly restoration including 'twelve thrones judging twelve tribes.' The finding about Jesus teaching some will 'live to see the kingdom of God come with power' and sit on thrones restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom theology. The specific argument that suffering functions as 'perfecting' required by God before the kingdom can 'fully appear' — establishing a causal link between suffering and political-theological legitimacy — is a specific theological mechanism not present in ch3's existing evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) covers Jesus teaching Greek Platonic model of immortal soul. Tabor documenting how Greek ideas of soul immortality were merged into Jewish-Christian theology restates the argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 30 (lines 535-544) explicitly covers 'Earliest Jesus images: Good Shepherd in pose of Hermes Criophorus (ram-bearing god).' The finding about early Christians representing Jesus as identical to Hermes Criophorus, comprising majority of earliest portrayals, restates evidence already present.

The argument that 'Sign of Jonah' was a recognized astrological event in antiquity, which Jesus explicitly referenced (Matthew/Luke) and everyone already understood, is not present in any chapter inventory. No chapter discusses the Sign of Jonah as an astrological concept. This is both a new argument and new evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Christos as Greek word meaning 'anointed.' The finding about Theophilus of Antioch (c. 180 CE) explaining Christos means 'anointed' and connecting it to ritual anointing ('we are called Christians because we are anointed with the oil of God') restates the anointing-title argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 19 covers Christ as anointing title, and item 17 covers baptism with anointing in the priestly co-heir section. The finding about anointing as central ceremonial practice in early Christianity (baptismal anointing, healing rites) restates the anointing significance already present.

Ch3 discusses resurrection implicitly through kingdom theology. Tabor explicitly identifying Paul's earliest resurrection views as 'apotheosis' — Greek theological term for transformation into glorified immortal state — adds the specific Hellenistic theological term as evidence for the Greco-Christian framework argument not present in ch3.

Ch3 does not discuss the Romulus apotheosis narrative in detail. Tabor providing Romulus as direct Hellenistic parallel — body rising and melting away — as Paul's model for resurrection adds specific comparative evidence not in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 17 covers baptism as priestly initiation. The specific archaeological evidence from John the Baptist's cave — first-century anointing of the right foot with oil as integral to baptism practice — is a specific archaeological datum not in ch3's evidence about baptism/anointing.

Ch3 inventory item 32 (lines 553-562) covers Kyrios as standard imperial honorific with detailed evidence. Tabor explaining kyrios in Greco-Roman social context as referring to masters, teachers, rulers rather than deity — with Abraham's wife as kyria — restates the existing argument about Kyrios as social/political title.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) covers Jesus teaching Greek Platonic soul doctrine. The concept of apotheosis — becoming like a god, becoming divinized — is covered in the chapter's framework of Hellenistic divine titles and the discussion of Son of God as Hellenistic royal title (item 20). Tabor's general discussion of apotheosis restates arguments already present.

Ch3 inventory item 6 (lines 51-53) covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula with the baptism as one of three events. The finding about the Isaiah servant voice at baptism being turned into 'this is my beloved Son' restates the baptism-as-investiture argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers Jesus and John as co-partners with 'John 3:22-23: two leaders baptizing simultaneously — paired ministry.' Tabor's reconstruction of 'the joint work of the two Messiahs now lost to Christian history' restates the dual-messiah argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution with earthly restoration. The finding about 'Kingdom of God on Earth is what Jesus inaugurated' restates the political kingdom argument already present.

Ch3 discusses the Greco-Christian framework generally. The specific evidence that Paul understood his resurrection experience as seeing 'the glorified Jesus, the Heavenly Christ with all of his power' (not a resuscitated corpse), and that later gospels progressively added physical resurrection, is specific evidence about the development of resurrection theology not present in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) covers Jesus teaching Greek Platonic model of soul. The finding about Paul using Platonic philosophical categories (body as 'tent,' 'old clothing') while synthesizing with Jewish emphasis on body restates the Platonic influence argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) covers Son of God as Hellenistic ruler title. The finding that Paul identifies Jesus as 'the first born of a family of beings called sons of God' — a cosmic rank creating 'a new rank of beings' — adds specific Pauline cosmic-christological evidence for the Son of God title not present in ch3.

Ch3 does not discuss the Talpiot ossuary with its Greek inscription using 'upso' (to lift up/exalt) in a resurrection context. The specific archaeological evidence — a Greek exaltation term on an ossuary near the Jesus family tomb, paired with Jonah imagery — is new evidence for the Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 inventory item 21 (lines 476-485) covers divine conception parallels including Plutarch's dynamis/pneuma and Luke 1:35. The finding about virgin birth arising as Hellenistic literary device to exalt Jesus as divine restates the argument already present in ch3's Son of Father section.

Ch3's framework argues for Greco-Christian royal theology. The finding about the Nicene Creed focusing on theological 'entrance and exit points' (virgin birth, resurrection, pre-existence, ascension) while omitting Jesus's teachings illustrates how Greco-Christian metaphysical theology displaced the kingdom message — a specific observation about institutional theology not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss Jesus within Orphic mystery traditions. The finding connecting Jesus to Orpheus, Dionysus, and Mithras through Hellenistic cosmological frameworks — with Orpheus combining 'kingly father, divine son, avian Holy Spirit' from three mystery traditions — is specific comparative evidence not in ch3.

The specific argument that eucharistic theology of consuming Jesus's body and blood originates from Greco-Roman magical sources rather than Jewish tradition — with Tabor noting 'eating human flesh and drinking blood, even symbolically, is completely alien to Judaism' — is not present in any chapter inventory. Ch4 item 15 covers John omitting the Eucharist; ch3 does not discuss eucharistic origins. This is a new argument with new evidence.

Ch3 does not explicitly discuss Paul's resurrection theology as apotheosis or compare it to Romulus. Tabor's explicit comparison of Paul's resurrection theology to the Romulus narrative — body transcending, transformation, taken to heaven — adds specific Hellenistic comparative evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 inventory item 20 covers Son of God as Hellenistic ruler title. The finding that Paul describes resurrection as transformation into 'sons of God' status — 'firstborn of many brothers and sisters' with a 'glorious body' reminiscent of 'Hellenistic accounts' — adds specific Pauline cosmic christology evidence not in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers the DSS dual messiah with Malachi references, two messianic figures, priestly and royal roles. The finding about DSS Malachi 3:1-4 with two figures ('the lord/adon' and 'messenger of the covenant') restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 covers John the Baptist's role with Josephus Ant. 18.116-119. The Slavonic Josephus account portraying John's message in explicitly political-institutional terms — 'summoned them to freedom,' 'free yourselves from many masters,' 'a king who had set them free' — is a specific textual source not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Christos as Greek word meaning 'anointed' with the Hasmoneans as anointed priest-kings. Tabor explaining the anointing ritual with oil to confirm priest or king restates the argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 17 (lines 345-421) covers the DSS dual messiah concept with Davidic and priestly messiahs. The finding about Davidic messiah expected to 'rule and reign' and priestly messiah from Aaron restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 41-42 covers kingdom as political institution. The finding about the unified message of John/Jesus/James centered on 'kingdom of god on earth' being 'lost and forgotten' as Christianity 'developed in a different direction' restates the political kingdom argument already in ch3.

Ch3 does not discuss Mark's original ending lacking resurrection appearances. The specific manuscript evidence that resurrection appearances were absent from Mark's original ending — appearing only in later gospels — is new evidence supporting the argument that resurrection theology was a deliberate development rather than original to the earliest tradition.

Ch3 inventory item 41 (lines 691-728) covers basileia as Hellenistic political term with extensive OT evidence. The finding about Isaiah's eschatological vision (Isaiah 2 and 11) with a Davidic figure exercising judgment across nations restates the political-institutional kingdom argument with OT sources already implicit in ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 45 (lines 757-835) covers collective sin and kingdom restoration. But the specific theodicy argument — how can a just Creator exclude the dead from the perfected future? — as the driving force behind resurrection theology development is a specific theological-logical argument not present in ch3's existing evidence.

Walsh identifying Paul as a 'middle platonist' educated in Platonic and Stoic philosophy concerns Pauline theology. This belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43: Paul employs Greek cosmology). Ch3 discusses Platonic influence generally but Paul's specific philosophical education is ch5 material.
Walsh explaining pneuma as physical substance within Stoic cosmology in Paul's usage belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43). Same content as CilDiHv4gzk_1 finding.

Walsh and Tabor discussing Paul's Middle Platonist cosmological framework (third heaven, souls shooting through atmosphere) concerns Pauline theology. This belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 43). Ch3 does not discuss Paul's cosmological framework.

Walsh documenting systematic theological mistranslation of Greek terms in NT contexts (pistis genitive changed from objective to subjective for Jesus) concerns Pauline textual interpretation. This belongs in chapter 5 (ch5 item 44). Same content as CilDiHv4gzk_3.

Walsh comparing Paul and Philo as comparable Middle Platonist thinkers claiming Jewish scriptures preceded Greek philosophy concerns Pauline theology. This belongs in chapter 5, which covers Paul's Hellenistic context. Ch3 discusses Philo's Logos but not Paul-Philo as parallel Middle Platonist thinkers.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers 'King of the Jews' as charge for execution and Messiah as political title. The finding about Jesus being executed by Rome 'for claiming to be a king' with charges 'purely political, not religious' restates evidence already present.

Ch3 does not discuss the Talpiot tomb discoveries linking earliest resurrection belief to proximity to Jesus's tomb. The specific archaeological evidence — earliest testimony of resurrection faith predating NT gospels found 200 feet from the purported tomb — supports understanding resurrection as theological/political rather than physical claim. This is new archaeological evidence.

Ch3 inventory item 20 (lines 465-474) covers Son of God as standard Hellenistic ruler title. Tabor's analysis that every king of Israel was 'son of God' but Christianity made it an 'exclusive designation' restates the royal title argument already present.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers Christos as 'anointed' with anointing for priests and kings. Tabor emphasizing Christ means 'anointed one' for both priests and kings restates evidence already present.

Ch2 inventory item 41 covers Herod's persecution of Davidic claimants. Ch3 inventory item 19 covers 'King of the Jews' as political title. The finding about Jesus's kingship claim as 'realistic threat to the powers of Rome' with Davidic bloodline feared by Herod restates arguments across ch2 and ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 10 (lines 127-144) covers Jesus teaching Greek Platonic soul doctrine. Tabor documenting how resurrection theology entered Judaism through Greek philosophical sources (Enoch texts, Plato) with Paul conceiving resurrection as 'spiritual body' transformation restates the Platonic influence argument already present.

Ch2 inventory item 38 covers Mary as royal descent and Theotokos. Ch3 inventory item 50 covers Mary with Greek royal imagery. The finding about Mary's priestly and royal lineage combined ('double royalty') restates evidence already present across ch2 and ch3.

Ch2 inventory item 52 covers dynastic succession of Jesus's brothers (James, Simon, Jude) into leadership. The finding about James's succession in dynastic terms restates evidence already present in ch2.

Ch3 discusses resurrection implicitly through kingdom theology. Tabor's explicit identification of Paul's resurrection as Greek apotheosis — 'a mortal kind of melts away and the immortal is glorified and emerges' — adds the specific Greek theological term and mechanism as evidence for the existing framework argument. Multiple findings bring this same apotheosis term; it remains new evidence for ch3.

Ch3 inventory item 19 (lines 445-464) covers kingship claims as fatal in Roman context. The finding about kingship claims being 'punishable by death' and that 'that's actually what got Jesus killed' restates evidence already present.

Ch3 inventory item 42 (lines 697-728) covers kingdom as earthly sovereignty restoration with various NT passages. But the specific argument that Jesus's 'cryptic remarks about being raised on the third day' refers to 'the resurrection of Israel the nation, not to Jesus's own individual Resurrection' is a NEW argument (national resurrection vs individual resurrection) using evidence already present in the book (Jesus's third-day sayings). The evidence exists but the interpretive conclusion is different.

Ch3 does not explicitly describe Mark's resurrection as apotheosis with the specific term 'lifted up' and 'taken up to the heavens.' The finding about Mark's account using Greek apotheosis language rather than physical resurrection adds specific Markan-Hellenistic evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 inventory item 6 (lines 51-53) covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula at baptism with western text of Luke 3:22 ('You are my Son, today I have begotten you'). Ch3 item 17 covers baptism as messianic anointing. The finding about heavens opening and 'you are my son' as divine designation paralleling royal acclamation restates the baptism-as-investiture argument already present.
